movement_definition: Stand with a dumbbell in each hand and the elbows close to the torso. Curl one dumbbell toward the shoulder while supinating the forearm, lower it under control, and alternate arms without swinging.
Stand with your knees slightly bent and your back straight. Hold a dumbbell in each hand, using a neutral grip at your sides.

Sitting on a bench, grab a barbell with your palms facing down and your hands shoulder-width apart. Rest your forearms on your thighs and allow your wrists to hang over your knees. Curl your knuckles towards your face, lifting the barbell. Pause for a moment in the top position, then slowly return the barbell to the starting position.
Stand or sit with a barbell held overhead in a narrow overhand grip. Keep the upper arms beside the head, bend the elbows to lower the bar behind you, then extend the elbows to return overhead.

Sitting on a bench, grab a barbell with your palms facing up and your hands shoulder-width apart. Rest your forearms on your thighs and allow your wrists to hang over your knees. Perform the movement by curling your palms and wrists towards your face. Pause for a moment in the top position, then slowly return the barbell to the starting position.
Flex the elbow against resistance in Bayesian Cable Curl while keeping the upper arm controlled, then return through full elbow extension.

Sit with the upper arms supported on the machine pad and align the elbows with its pivot. Curl the handles by flexing the elbows, pause briefly, then lower under control without lifting the upper arms.
Stand holding a barbell with a shoulder-width supinated grip. Keep the elbows near the torso, curl the bar toward the shoulders without swinging, then lower it under control.
Stand with a dumbbell in each hand and the elbows close to the torso. Curl both dumbbells toward the shoulders, supinating the forearms as needed, then lower them under control without swinging.
Stand holding an EZ bar with a comfortable supinated grip. Keep the upper arms still, curl the bar toward the shoulders, then lower it under control without using torso momentum.
Grab the handles of the TRX straps, lean your body back, arms and legs extended, with your body positioned in a single straight line. (This is an arm exercise, not an abdominal one.).

Hold a fixed bar with a supinated grip and walk the feet forward so the rigid body leans back beneath it. Keep the elbows high, flex them to pull the forehead toward the bar, then extend under control without bending at the hips.

The concentration curl is a classic exercise for building the biceps one arm at a time. It can be performed bent over or kneeling, but is more often performed seated on a bench. It's great for emphasizing the biceps peak and is often used to finish a biceps workout.
Hold a cable handle near the opposite shoulder with the elbow bent. Keep the upper arm controlled and extend the elbow diagonally across the body, then return without using torso rotation.
Grab onto the handle, and pull your elbow up and back slightly above your torso. Keep your upper arm to be parallel to the ground for the duration of the set.
Begin with one cable handle near the chest, the elbow bent and slightly flared. Press the handle forward and down by extending the elbow, then return under control while keeping the shoulder stable.

Sit at the front of a stable chair with both hands gripping the edge. Move the hips forward, bend the elbows to lower through a comfortable range, then extend the elbows to press back up while keeping the chair fixed.

Setup: Lie flat on the bench and grip the bar at shoulder-width (closer than a standard bench press). Descent: Lower the bar with control to your lower chest. Form: Keep your elbows tucked in tight against your torso—do not let them flare out. Ascent: Press the bar straight back up, squeezing your triceps at the top.

Drop into a strong plank position, bringing your hands close together until they're almost touching. (picture 1)Bend your elbows to slowly bring your chest to the floor (picture 2). Keep your elbows close to your body as you push back up explosively. Repeat. Ensure you take your time lowering on each rep, keeping your form sharp.
Place elbow so that it is about level with the shoulder. Curl the wieght to your shoulder. Focus on the stretch in the eccentric part of the curl.

Stand upright and grip the kettlebell with both hands. Perform the elbow flexion motion, starting from a fully extended position until your hand reaches shoulder height. Spread your legs a little for stability and, to perform the exercise correctly, try not to push with your back or body in general. Change the weight of the kettlebell to adjust the difficulty.
Dumbbell cross body biceps curls, works on the braccialis.

Sit with a dumbbell in each hand, palms down, and rest the forearms across the thighs with the wrists beyond the knees. Lower the knuckles through controlled wrist flexion, then extend the wrists to raise the dumbbells without lifting the forearms.
Dead Hang performed on an edge either with or without added weight (adujst edge or weight to adjust difficulty).

Begin in a rigid plank with the hands close together beneath the chest so the thumbs and index fingers form a diamond. Lower the chest while keeping the elbows close to the torso, then press back to straight arms without letting the hips sag.

Put two benches so far appart, that you can hold onto one with your hands and are just able to reach the other with your feet. The legs stay during the exercise completely stretched. With your elbows facing back, bend them as much as you can. Push yourself up, but don't stretch out the arms.

Face a high cable with a rope attachment and hinge forward slightly. Keep the elbows just behind the torso, extend them while drawing the rope down beside the body, then return without letting the shoulders roll forward.
Bend your elbows and lift the dumbbells to your shoulders. Push the dumbbells over your head while rotating your arms to make your palms face forward. Reverse it to lower down. Repeat the exercise.

Stand with a dumbbell in each hand and use a small, deliberate hip drive to initiate the curl. Finish by flexing the elbows, then emphasize a slow controlled lowering phase while keeping the trunk braced.

Lie down with your back on a flat bench. Hold a dumbbell in each hand. Raise your arms towards the ceiling with your palms facing each other and dumbbells pressed together. Slowly lower the dumbbells to your chest, then slowly push them back. Repeat the exercise.
Sit on bench. Grasp dumbbell between feet. Place back of upper arm to inner thigh. Lean into leg to raise elbow slightly.
Hold a dumbbell in each hand with your palms facing forward. Pull your elbows back and lift the dumbbells to your chest height. Slowly lower the dumbbell and repeat the exercise.
Walk with a dumbbell at each side while keeping the grip closed, shoulders level, and trunk upright.

Sit against an incline bench with a dumbbell in each hand and the arms hanging behind the torso. Keep the upper arms still, curl the weights toward the shoulders, then lower to full controlled elbow extension.
Hold a dumbbell in each hand with your hands slightly wider than shoulder-width apart and palms facing forward. Bend your elbows and lift the dumbbells to your shoulders. Slowly return and repeat.
Sit at a Scott or preacher station with the upper arms supported and curl the dumbbells without lifting the elbows from the pad.

Lie on a bench with dumbbells above the chest and palms facing forward. Keep the upper arms stable as the elbows flare and bend to bring the dumbbells toward the chest, then extend the elbows to return.
Grip a 20 mm fingerboard edge with both hands and hang with the shoulders engaged for the prescribed duration. Use assistance or added load only as programmed.
Sit with the hands behind the hips, fingers facing forward, and knees bent. Lift the hips, bend the elbows to lower the body toward the floor, then extend the elbows to return without shrugging.
Allowing you to safely load more weight than a regular skull crusher, lie on the floor and rest the weight on the floor in between each repetition.

Start: Hold dumbbells at your sides with palms facing your torso. Curl: Lift the weights toward your shoulders while maintaining the neutral grip (like holding a hammer). Squeeze: Contract the biceps at the top without moving your elbows forward. Lower: Slowly return to the starting position with full control.

Take a cable in your hands (palms parallel, point to each other), the body is straight. Bend the arms and bring the weight up with a fast movement. Without any pause bring it back down with a slow, controlled movement, but don't stretch completely your arms. Don't swing your body during the exercise, the biceps should do all the work here. The elbows are at your side and don't move.
Hold a hand gripper in one palm, squeeze the handles together by flexing the fingers, and reopen them under control without bending the wrist.

The high-cable cross tricep extension is an isolation exercise for targeting the triceps, particularly the long head. It utilizes cables and handles grasped with opposite hands, allowing for a unique pressing motion that can be effective for building tricep strength. Unlike exercises that use a straight bar or rope, this variation can be more comfortable on your wrists and elbows.
Narrower grip than regular bench press, just outside shoulder width.

Lie against an incline bench with a dumbbell in each hand above the shoulders. Keep the upper arms nearly still, bend the elbows to lower the dumbbells beside the head, then extend the elbows to return.

The JM press keeps your shoulders stationary, relying on the elbow flexion and extension to move the weight using your triceps strength. That means you get all the benefits of the best triceps-strengthening exercises without overtaxing that delicate shoulder joint. Classic win-win!

Lie face-up on a bench with the arms hanging beside the torso and a dumbbell in each hand. Keep the upper arms still, curl the weights toward the shoulders, then lower them through a controlled range.

Lie chest-down on an incline bench with the upper arms held beside the torso and elbows bent. Extend the elbows until the arms are straight, pause, then lower the dumbbells without moving the shoulders.

Set the pulley at the bottom of the cable machine and grab onto it without using any attachments. Extend the cable directly overhead. While keeping your back straight and upper arm stationary, lower the cable behind your head until you feel a good stretch in your triceps, and then extend it back upward until your elbow is locked out.

Standing with the kettlebell in one hand and bent at the elbow, start from a fully extended position until your hand reaches shoulder height. To perform the movement correctly, try not to push with your back or body.
Hands at shoulder height, curl arms in toward head, then back out.
Face away from a low cable with a rope held overhead and the elbows bent. Keep the upper arms near the head, extend the elbows until the arms are straight, then return under control.

Pinch one or more weight plates between the fingers and thumb of each hand. Stand tall with the shoulders relaxed and hold the plates for the prescribed duration without supporting them against the body.
Sit at a preacher bench with one upper arm supported and rotate the torso slightly away from the working arm. Curl the dumbbell without lifting the arm from the pad, then lower under control.
Sit at a preacher bench with one upper arm supported and rotate the torso slightly toward the working arm. Curl the dumbbell without lifting the arm from the pad, then lower under control.
Support the upper arms on a preacher pad and begin with the elbows nearly extended. Curl the bar toward the shoulders without lifting the arms from the pad, then lower through a controlled range.
Stand with a pronated grip on the bar, flex the elbows to curl it toward the shoulders without moving the upper arms, and lower to full elbow extension.
Flex the elbow against resistance in Reverse-Grip EZ-Bar Cable Biceps Curl while keeping the upper arm controlled, then return through full elbow extension.
Sitting reverse on a Biceps Bench with a close grip.
Support the body between two rings, lower by flexing the elbows and shoulders, and press back to straight arms while controlling ring movement.

Hold yourself in a support position on gymnastic rings with arms straight at sides. Focus on keeping rings turned out slightly and maintaining hollow body position. This is an isometric hold for chest, shoulders, and triceps.

Set your feet below the rope and lean your upper body towards the rope. Set one foot back. Push down the rope and lean your entire body back onto the rear foot as you push down to get your hands behind your body. Don't forget to bring your chest to the front during the pushdown. As you release the rope slowly, lean your body back onto the front foot.

Seated Dumbbell Biceps Curl are an effective bicep workout that isolates the muscles by stabilizing the upper body, reducing momentum that can detract from the exercise’s effectiveness. This exercise is performed sitting down with a dumbbell in each hand, focusing on controlled movement to maximize engagement of the bicep muscles.

Sit down on a back (better with back support). Take a dumbbell firmly with both hands and hold it with extended arms over your head. With your palms facing upward and holding the weight of the dumbbell, slowly lower the weight behind your head.
Sit on bench with 60° incline Hold dumbbells beside body near the ground, palms facing outwards, arms fully extended Move dumbbells up using only biceps and without momentum.
Set a cable above shoulder height and hold one handle with the elbow tucked at the side. Extend the elbow until the arm is straight, then return under control without letting the upper arm drift.
Sit on the preacher curl bench and perform a bicep curl with a dumbbell in one hand. Your other hand can be at rest, or beneath your curling arm's elbow.
Lie on a bench with dumbbells above the shoulders. Keep the upper arms nearly still, bend the elbows to lower the weights beside the head, then extend the elbows to return.

Hold the SZ-bar and lay down on a flat bench in such a way that around 1/4 of your head is over the edge. Stretch your arms with the bar and bend them so that the bar is lowered. Just before it touches your forehead, push it up. Pay attention to your elbows and arms: only the triceps are doing the work, the rest of the arms should not move.
Hanging on sloper holds of a fingerboard for a amount of seconds.
Perform a standard bench press on the smith machine, but have your hands on the bar about shoulder width apart, and keep your elbows close to your body.
Lie chest-down on an incline bench with the arms hanging vertically. Curl the weights without moving the upper arms or lifting the chest, pause briefly, then lower under control.

Hold a wrist-roller bar at about chest height with a weight suspended from its rope. Alternately flex and extend the wrists to wind the rope up, then reverse the motion to lower the weight under control.

Stand facing a low cable and hold the straight bar with a shoulder-width supinated grip. Keep the upper arms beside the torso, curl the bar toward the shoulders, then lower to controlled elbow extension without swinging.

Setup: Stand facing the cable, feet shoulder-width apart, with a slight forward lean. Grip: Hold the rope with palms facing each other and elbows tucked tight to your ribs. Execution: Push the rope down until arms are fully straight, pulling the ends apart at the bottom. Control: Keep upper arms still and return to the start with a slow, controlled motion.

Grab the bar, stand with your feet shoulder wide, keep your back straight and lean forward a little. Push the bar down, making sure the elbows don't move during the exercise. Without pause go back to the initial position.

Sit down and hold the bar firmly with your hands. Now press the weight upwards (don't fully extend your arms) and lower it slowly again. As with other triceps exercises, it's important not to move your upper arms.

Stand tall and hold one dumbbell overhead with both hands. Keep the upper arms near the ears, bend the elbows to lower the dumbbell behind the head, then extend the elbows without flaring the upper arms.

Start with the hands on the suspension trainer and feet on the ground. Your hands should be under your shoulders with the feet slightly in front. Lower yourself by bringing the hips between the handles and flexing the elbows. Extend the elbows to come back to the starting position and repeat.
Face the suspension anchor with a neutral grip and lean back in a rigid body line. Keep the elbows high and wide, flex them to bring the forehead toward the handles, then extend under control.

Place the grips as if they were a continuation of the straps. We will position our arms stretched forward and our body leaning back, completely straight. The biceps muscles will activate as you bend your elbows to bring your body towards the straps. The contraction movement will end with a slight outward movement of the hands, due to the grip we are using.

Step 1: Stand comfortably and grasp the handles of the suspension system. Step 2: Lean back resting your weight on one arm, keepng the spine neutral. Step 3: Slowly curl to pull your body weight to the up position. Supinate the arm and squeeze as you approach the end position. Step 4: Lower yourself back down and repeat.

Face away from the suspension anchor with the arms extended and body inclined. Bend the elbows to bring the forehead toward the hands, then extend the elbows to return while keeping the trunk and upper arms stable.

Extend your arm down, then slightly lift your forearm Extend your wrist in a supinated form (palm up) Adjust cable height so that the cable handle is in your supinated hand Perform the movement by curling your palms and wrists upwards (optional/variable) Pause at the top Slowly return to the starting position.

Sitting on a bench, grab the dumbbell with your palms facing up and your hands shoulder-width apart. Rest your forearms on your thighs and allow your wrists to hang over your knees. Perform the movement by curling your palms and wrists towards your face. (optional/variable) Pause at the top. Slowly return to the starting position.

Stand with dumbbells at the sides and palms forward. Curl the weights toward the shoulders, rotate to a palms-down grip at the top, lower slowly, then rotate the palms forward before the next repetition.
Attach a D-Handle to a high pully. And use your lat muscles to pull the weight single handedly.

Hold one high cable handle with the arm extended and stand in a stable split stance. Pull the elbow down and back toward the ribs, return under control, and alternate or complete the prescribed work on each side.
Slightly bent down and knees to mimic a gorilla position and pull/row up the DBs to the chest. DB stay straight (thumbs pointing up).
Hang from a wide grip, pull toward one hand while the opposite arm straightens along the bar, then lower under control. Alternate sides while keeping the trunk braced.

Lie supine with the feet planted and hands beside the ears, fingers pointing toward the shoulders. Press through the hands and feet into a comfortable full bridge and hold the arched position for the prescribed duration.
Hanging from a pull-up bar facing downwards with your arms extended.

Hold the band with your arms extended in front of you at shoulder height. Keep your elbows slightly bent but fixed throughout the movement. Pull the band apart by spreading your arms until the band touches or comes close to your chest. Slowly bring your arms back to the starting position, controlling the tension on the band.

Anchor a resistance band at shoulder height and hold it with the arm extended. Keep the torso still and draw the shoulder blade back toward the spine without shrugging, then let it protract under control.
Hinge forward with a braced spine and supinated grip; pull the bar toward the lower torso while keeping the elbows close, then lower to straight arms.

Stand with your feet shoulder width apart holding the barbell with both hands in front just past shoulder width. Bend forward at the hips with a slight bend in your knees, keeping your back straight. Engage your shoulder blades, as if you are trying to touch them together. Release the shrug. Description taken from MuscleWiki.

With dumbbells in hand, bend at the hip until hands hang just below the knees (similar to straight-legged-deadlift starting position). Keep upper body angle constant while contracting your lats to pull you ellbows back pinching the shoulder blades at the top. Try not to stand up with every rep, check hands go below knees on every rep.

exercise_name: Bent-Over Dumbbell Row to External Rotation
movement_definition: Hinge with the trunk supported or braced and raise the elbows to shoulder height. With the elbows bent about 90 degrees, rotate the forearms upward, reverse the rotation, and lower the arms under control.
Hinge over a barbell with a neutral spine and knees softly bent. Row the bar toward the lower ribs while holding the torso angle, then lower it until the arms are extended.
Lie on stomach, intertwine fingers behind the head and start raising your head and raise elbows as far as they go.
Attach two D-Handles to two low cables and shrug in using your upper traps.
Hang from a bar with a shoulder-width supinated grip. Pull until the chin clears the bar while keeping the shoulders controlled, then lower to a full hang without swinging.

Grip the pull-down bar with your hands closer than shoulder width apart, with your palms facing away from you. Lean back slightly. Pull the bar down towards your chest, keeping your elbows close to your sides as you come down. Pull your shoulders back at the end of the motion.
Hang beside the bar with one hand in a supinated grip and the other in a pronated grip. Pull the head past one side of the bar without twisting the trunk, lower under control, and alternate sides.
Attach a D-Bar to the cable machine. Perform a motion akin to drawing a sword from across your body.

Stand with the bar over midfoot, hinge to grip it, and brace the trunk with the spine neutral. Push through the floor and extend the knees and hips to stand tall without leaning back, then return the bar by hinging under control.
Stand on a low stable platform with the bar over midfoot. Brace, hinge, and bend the knees to grip the bar, then extend the knees and hips to stand tall before returning the bar under control.
Lie on a bench holding one dumbbell above the chest with slightly bent elbows. Lower the weight in an arc behind the head while keeping the ribs controlled, then pull it back above the chest.

This exercise involves holding a dumbbell in each hand and shrugging your shoulders up towards your ears, then lowering them back down. It primarily targets the trapezius muscles in the upper back and can help improve posture and shoulder stability.
Hinge over two dumbbells with a supinated grip, neutral spine, and softly bent knees. Row the weights toward the hips while holding the torso angle, then lower them until the arms are extended.

Begin in a front-lever position with the body horizontal and arms straight. Bend the elbows to pull the chest toward the bar while maintaining the lever, then lower to straight arms without letting the hips drop.

Hang from a bar and draw the knees tightly toward the chest. Depress the shoulders and extend the hips until the tucked torso is horizontal, then hold the position without bending the elbows or letting the hips sag.

Face a high cable from a half-kneeling stance and begin with one arm extended. Pull the handle diagonally toward the upper ribs by driving the elbow down and back, then return without rotating the torso.
Hold the top position of an inverted row with the chest drawn toward the bar, shoulder blades retracted, and body in a straight line.

Set the hips against a back-extension pad with the feet secured and the torso hinged forward. Extend the hips until the torso aligns with the legs without overarching the lower back, then lower under control.
Lie chest-down on an incline bench with a dumbbell in each hand. Row the weights toward the lower ribs while retracting the shoulder blades, then lower until the arms are extended.
Maintain a straight body, retract your shoulder blades, and pull your chest to the bar for an effective back and upper body workout.
Begin in a high plank with one dumbbell outside a hand. Reach across with the opposite hand and drag the dumbbell beneath the torso without rotating the pelvis, then alternate sides.
Row one kettlebell toward the torso, rotate the trunk under control, and reverse the sequence to return.

Stand over a kettlebell in a wide stance, hinge and bend the knees to grip it, then explosively extend the hips and knees. Guide the bell upward with high elbows before lowering it safely to the floor.

Begin on hands and knees, brace the trunk, and extend one arm forward with the opposite leg backward. Hold the aligned position for the prescribed duration without rotating the pelvis, then change sides.

Support one hand on a stable surface and hold a heavy dumbbell in the other. Use a controlled amount of torso movement to row the dumbbell toward the hip, then lower to a full stretch while maintaining a braced stance.
L-Sit Pull-Up train both upper body and core muscles.

Hinge forward with a dumbbell in each hand and the arms hanging beneath the shoulders. Keep the elbows nearly fixed as you sweep the weights backward toward the hips, then return them under control without turning the motion into a row.
The lat pull down is an exercise used to build the muscles of the back. While the exercise will primarily target the lats, you will also notice a fair amount of bicep and middle back activation.
Pull the overhead handle toward the upper torso in Leaning-Back Lat Pulldown while depressing the shoulder blades, then return under control.
Pull the overhead handle toward the upper torso in Upright Lat Pulldown while depressing the shoulder blades, then return under control.

Sit diagonally at a lat-pulldown station with one thigh secured and the opposite hand on the handle. Pull the elbow down and across the torso toward the hip, then return to an overhead stretch without rotating the trunk.
Adjust seat height so that the handles are at the bottom of your pectorals or just below.

Sit down, put your feet on the supporting points and grab the bar with a wide grip. Pull the weight with a rapid movement towards your belly button, not upper. Keep your arms and elbows during the movement close to your body. Your shoulders are pulled together. Let the weight slowly down till your arms are completely stretched.

The Meadows row is a unilateral row performed with a landmine setup, overhand grip, and staggered stance. Lean your torso forward and grip the barbell. Rest the other forearm on the forward leg. Start this movement by driving the elbow behind you while retracting the shoulder-blade. Keep the working shoulder down. Pull toward your back hip until the elbow is level with your torso.

Hang from a bar, pull explosively until the chest rises above it, transition the torso over the bar, and press to straight-arm support. Reverse the transition under control before returning to a hang.
Pull the overhead handle toward the upper torso in Neutral-Grip Lat Pulldown while depressing the shoulder blades, then return under control.
Pull the load toward the torso in Single-Arm Dumbbell Row by driving the elbow back and controlling the shoulder blade, then extend the arm.

Back excercise with a barbell with a starting position which is in a bent over position with the back paralell to the ground. The barbell is on the ground at chest level.For the movement grab the barbell at shoulder width grip and pull towards your chest without losing the bent over position and without moving anything but your arms.

exercise_name: Prone Scapular Retraction with Arms at Sides
movement_definition: Lying on stomach with head on towel. Stretch arms straight out to your sides. Slowly lift your arms, pulling your shoulderblades together, hold for 3 seconds.
Pull until the chin clears the bar and hold that flexed-arm position for the prescribed duration. Keep the shoulders controlled and lower safely when the hold ends.
Hang from a bar with a shoulder-width pronated grip. Pull until the chin clears the bar while keeping the shoulders controlled and trunk braced, then lower to a full hang without swinging.

Hang from parallel handles with the palms facing each other. Drive the elbows down and back to pull the chest toward the hands until the chin reaches handle height, then lower to a full hang under control without shrugging.

Execution Pulling phase (concentric): Drive your elbows down and slightly back , pulling your chest toward the bar . Focus on leading with your chest , not your chin. Keep your shoulders depressed (avoid shrugging). Top position: Chin should clear the bar (or at least reach bar level). Pause for a brief squeeze in your lats .
Move the load from overhead toward the torso in Machine Pullover with nearly fixed elbows, then return through shoulder flexion.
Pullup on a choosen edge of a fingerboard / hangboard.

Deadlift to be done using a Smith machine or a free rack. Bar or barbell hould be just right under the knee cap level. Lift using the glutes and through the heels, then come back to starting postion with a control movement of 2 seconds. This exercise targets mainly the lower back and glutes.

Hold a high plank on two dumbbells with the feet set wide for balance. Row one dumbbell toward the ribs without rotating the pelvis, lower it, and alternate arms; this record does not include a push-up.
With hands behind the torso, extend the hips until shoulders, pelvis, and feet form a straight line while maintaining shoulder support.
Lay flat on your stomach with your arms extended in front of you on the ground as your legs are lying flat. Lift both your arms and move them to your side slowly. Then, move them back.
Pull the load toward the torso in Chest-Supported Barbell Row by driving the elbow back and controlling the shoulder blade, then extend the arm.
Hang straight on a pull-up bar Pull shoulder blades together, moving the body slightly up.
Elevate the shoulder blades against resistance in Seated Cable Mid-Trap Shrug without bending the elbows, then lower under control.
Sit at a cable row with the trunk tall and arms extended. Pull the handle toward the lower ribs while bringing the shoulder blades together, then return under control without rocking the torso.
Keep your chest up and squeeze your shoulder blades at the end. Don’t hunch forward or round your back during the pull.

The Seated V-Grip Cable Row is a compound pulling exercise performed on a cable machine using a close-grip "V" handle. While seated with your feet braced, you pull the handle toward your midsection, focusing on back thickness and mid-back development.

Hold a low cable handle in one hand from a split stance with the arm extended. Retract the shoulder blade and row the handle toward the ribs, optionally supinating the forearm, then return under control.
Stand between low pulleys and hold the crossed cable handles in front of the thighs. Lead with the elbows to draw the handles upward along the torso, then lower under control without shrugging.
Elevate the shoulder blades against resistance in Smith Machine Shrug without bending the elbows, then lower under control.

Take a barbell and stand with a straight body, the arms are hanging freely in front of you. Lift from this position the shoulders as high as you can, but don't bend the arms during the movement. On the highest point, make a short pause of 1 or 2 seconds before returning slowly to the initial position. When training with a higher weight, make sure that you still do the whole movement!
Stand facing a high cable with one arm extended and the elbow softly bent. Sweep the handle down beside the hip while keeping the torso still, then return through controlled shoulder flexion.
Start position as row, extend to plank and back. Finish with row and repeat.
Lie prone with the arms extended in a T. Gently lift the chest and arms, draw the shoulder blades together without shrugging, hold the top position briefly, and lower under control.

Face a high cable and hold the straight bar with a pronated grip and softly bent elbows. Sweep the bar from shoulder height toward the thighs without bending the elbows further, then return under control without shrugging.
Face a high cable and hold the rope ends with the arms extended and elbows softly bent. Sweep the rope toward the hips while separating its ends slightly, then return under control without shrugging.

Lay flat on your stomach with your arms extended in front of you on the ground as your legs are lying flat. Lift both your arms and legs at the same time, as if you were flying, and contract the lower back. Make sure that you are breathing and, depending on your fitness level, hold the movement for at least two to five seconds per repetition.
Hinge over a T-bar or landmine handle with the trunk braced and spine neutral. Row the load toward the lower chest, bringing the shoulder blades together, then lower until the arms are extended.

In this exercise, you lie flat on your stomach, hold a towel with your arms extended in front of you, and pull it apart to create tension. Then, you move it forward and back under your stomach. This exercise targets the back and shoulder muscles as well as core stability.

Hinge forwards and bend your knees slightly. Depress and retract the shoulder you will use. Rest the other hand on your knee. Raise your hand about 15 degrees overhead, maintaining the position of your shoulder.
Pull the load toward the torso in TRX Row by driving the elbow back and controlling the shoulder blade, then extend the arm.
Pull until the chin is above the bar, then shift the upper body toward one hand and across toward the other while staying high. Return to center and lower under control before the next repetition.

Grip the pull-down bar with your palms facing you and your hands closer than shoulder-width apart. Lean back slightly and keep your back straight. Pull the bar down towards your chest, pulling your shoulders back slightly at the end of the motion.
With an inclined bench in front of the cable row machine, do onehanded rows with focus on a big stretch in the lats.
Sit at the upper-back row machine with the chest against the pad and handles near shoulder height. Drive the elbows back and retract the shoulder blades, then return the handles under control.

Starting position: Arms extended overhead, feeling a stretch in your lats. Keep your shoulders depressed (don’t shrug upward). Pulling phase (concentric): Pull the bar down to your upper chest or collarbone area by driving your elbows down and back. Keep your chest lifted and squeeze your shoulder blades together at the bottom.

Lie on the bench with the feet planted and shoulder blades set. Lower the bar to the mid-to-lower chest with the forearms controlled, then press it back over the shoulders; use safeties or a spotter for heavy attempts.

The movement is very similar to benchpressing with a barbell, however, the weight is brought down to the chest at a lower point. Hold two dumbbells and lay down on a bench. Hold the weights next to the chest, at the height of your nipples and press them up till the arms are stretched. Let the weight slowly and controlled down.
Hinge forward while holding opposing cable handles and sweep the arms across the chest through horizontal adduction with a nearly fixed elbow angle.

Single arm chest press done with the cable machine. Use the other arm to brace bodyweight to focus on strength of the press, rather than balancing of the body. Start with the hand as close to the chest as possible, and then press against the cable at a slight decline and aiming towards the center of your chest.

Single arm chest press done with the cable machine. Use the other arm to brace bodyweight to focus on strength of the press, rather than balancing of the body. Start with the hand as close to the chest as possible, and then press against the cable at a slight incline and aiming towards the center of your chest.

Set both cables above shoulder height and stand between them with soft elbows. Sweep the handles downward and inward toward the lower chest, squeeze briefly, then return along the same arc under control.
Set both cables near chest height and stand centered with soft elbows. Draw the handles together in front of the chest without turning the movement into a press, then open the arms under control.
Set both cables low and stand centered with soft elbows. Sweep the handles upward and inward toward upper-chest height, squeeze briefly, then return along the same arc under control.

Set one cable near chest height, stand angled away, and hold the handle beside the chest. Press the arm forward and inward in an arc across the torso, then return under control without rotating the trunk.

Begin in a rigid push-up position, lower the chest with control, then press explosively so both hands leave the floor. Clap only if there is time, replace the hands, and absorb the landing through bent elbows.
Underhand grip DB, perform a normal bench press movement, keeping the elbows close to the chest.

Lay down on a decline bench, the bar should be directly above your eyes, the knees are somewhat angled and the feet are firmly on the floor. Concentrate, breath deeply and grab the bar more than shoulder wide. Bring it slowly down till it briefly touches your chest at the height of your nipples. Push the bar up.

Take two dumbbells and sit on a decline bench, the feet are firmly on the floor, the head is resting the bench. Hold the weights next to the chest, at the height of your nipples and press them up till the arms are stretched. Let the weight slowly and controlled down.
With your feet raised approximately 30cm on a platform, align your shoulders, elbows and hands, then perform regular pushups. This emphasises the clavicular fibers of the pectoralis major.
Pushup on blocks or grips, so you can dip lower than the hands in the decent.
Support the body on parallel bars with the elbows extended. Lower by bending the elbows and shoulders through a comfortable range, then press back to support without bouncing.

Grab your dumbbells and lay flat on your back with your knees bent and your feet flat on the ground (use a bench if you have one). Press the weights up, locking out your elbows (A). Lower them slowly until your upper arms are resting on the floor (B) close to your body. Pause here before explosively pressing back up. Squeeze your chest hard and repeat.
Lie on a bench and squeeze two dumbbells together over the chest, lower them while maintaining inward pressure, and press to straight arms without separating the weights.

Grip two stable dumbbells beneath the shoulders and hold a rigid plank. Lower the chest between the handles through a controlled comfortable range, then press back to straight arms without letting the dumbbells move.
Elbows-Tucked Dumbbell Bench Press, chest press movement focusing on the triceps. DB stays parallel with body.

Begin in a plank on the fingertips and thumbs with the hands under the shoulders. Lower through a shallow controlled push-up and press back up while distributing pressure evenly; use the knees or reduced range as needed.

Take two dumbbells and lay on a bench, make sure the feet are firmly on the ground and your back is not arched, but has good contact with the bench. The arms are stretched in front of you, about shoulder wide. Bend now the arms a bit and let them down with a half-circle movement to the side. Without changing the angle of the elbow bring them in a fluid movement back up.

Lie on a decline bench with dumbbells above the chest and elbows softly bent. Open the arms in a wide arc through a comfortable range, then bring the dumbbells back together without changing elbow angle.

With one dumbbell in hand, perform a glute bridge. Now hold the dumbbell above your chest, this is the rep starting position. Lower the dumbbell so that your elbow touches the floor or is roughly 45 degrees below your shoulder if using a bench. Push the dumbbell back into the starting position.

Adjust the plate-loaded decline press so the handles begin near the lower chest. Keep the back against the pad, press the handles forward until the elbows are nearly extended, then return through a controlled comfortable range.
Set the bench to a 45-60° incline. Touch the upper chest with the bar.
Lower the resistance toward the chest in Incline Barbell Bench Press and press it away while keeping the shoulder blades supported.
Use inclined bench. Hold dumbbells straight out to your sides, elbows slightly bent. Bring arms together above you, keeping angle of elbows fixed.
Lie on a 30–45 degree incline with dumbbells beside the upper chest, press them upward to straight arms, and lower with the forearms kept under the weights.
Place the hands on an elevated support, lower the braced body toward it, and press back to straight arms.

Place your hands on the bench in a press up position Push your chest away from the bench to separate your shoulder blades Keeping your elbows straight , lower your chest towards the bench so your shoulder blades move closer together Push your chest away from the bench again.
Set a bench to a moderate 30–40 degree incline beneath the Smith bar. Lower the bar toward the upper chest with the forearms aligned, then press it upward along the guide rails without bouncing.

Hold the top of an incline press with the elbows softly extended, shoulder blades set, and trunk braced. Maintain the prescribed position and breathing for the full duration, then rack the load safely.
Stand facing a wall with both hands at chest height. Press firmly into the wall and maintain the same joint positions for the prescribed duration while breathing steadily.
Begin in a wide push-up position. Shift the chest toward one hand as that elbow bends, travel laterally across toward the other hand, then return to center and repeat for controlled repetitions.

Loop a resistance band across the upper back and secure its ends beneath the hands while gripping two stable, flat-based kettlebell handles. Lower the chest in a rigid plank, then press to straight arms against the band without letting the kettlebells tip.

Begin in a straight line from the knees through the shoulders with the hands under or just outside the shoulders. Lower the chest by bending the elbows, then press back up without hinging at the hips.
Put your legs up on a separate Bench and press with no leg drive.
Adjust the plate-loaded incline press so the handles begin beside the upper chest. Keep the back and shoulder blades supported, press the handles forward and upward, then return under control.
Adjust the plate-loaded leverage press so the handles align with the mid-chest. Keep the back supported, press the handles forward to nearly straight arms, then return under control.

Sit on the butterfly machine, the feet have a good contact with the floor, the upper arms are parallel to the floor. Press your arms together till the handles are practically together (but aren't!). Go slowly back. The weights should stay all the time in the air.
Adjust the seat and selectorized weight stack so the handles align with the mid-chest. Keep the back supported, press the handles forward to nearly straight arms, then return under control.
Set up for a normal Dumbbell chest press but lift your leg ups straight or put them on another bench to remove the leg drive.

Set the two cable handles at different heights and stand centered between them. With soft elbows, draw the hands across the body along the chosen diagonal, then return under control and repeat with the opposite setup.
Perform push-ups with one hand, alternating the sides.

Lie on a flat bench and lower the bar to the chest under control. Hold it motionless without relaxing for the prescribed pause, then press until the elbows extend and repeat with the same deliberate stop.
Set security pins to about the height of your sticking point Lower the bar, rest on the pins for 1s while holding tension Move bar up with maximum force.
Begin in a rigid plank with the hands about shoulder-width apart. Lower the chest while keeping the elbows controlled and body aligned, then press back to straight arms.
Perform a controlled push-up, then rotate into a side plank and reach the top arm toward the ceiling. Return the hand to the floor, complete the next push-up, and alternate rotation sides.
Hold the parallettes in a rigid plank position. Lower the chest between the handles through a controlled comfortable depth, then press back to straight arms without letting the hips sag.

Lie on a flat bench with the shoulder blades set and hold the bar in a shoulder-width supinated grip. Lower it toward the lower chest with the wrists stacked over the forearms, then press it back over the shoulders with a spotter or safeties in place.
Sit upright between cable handles with the arms opened and elbows softly bent. Bring the hands together in front of the chest without changing elbow angle, then open the arms under control.

Set up in a shoulder-width push-up position and lift one foot while keeping the pelvis level. Lower the chest by bending the elbows, press back to the top, and complete the prescribed work before changing the raised leg.

Begin in a wide, rigid push-up position. Shift the chest toward one hand as you lower, press back through center, then shift toward the other hand on the next repetition while keeping the pelvis level.
On a slightly inclined bench, lower the Smith-machine bar toward the upper chest and press it vertically along the guide rails.
From supine, explosively pass the medicine ball vertically from the chest and receive it with bent elbows.
Lie supine with a dumbbell over each shoulder and protract the shoulder blades, reaching the weights upward without bending the elbows.
Sweep the arms through the horizontal shoulder motion named by Suspension Chest Fly while keeping the elbow angle nearly fixed.
Lower the braced body toward the support in Wall Push-Up and press back to straight arms without losing trunk alignment.
Set up in a rigid plank with the hands wider than shoulder width. Lower the chest between the hands while keeping the elbows controlled, then press back to straight arms without letting the hips sag.

Starting Position : Kneel on the floor with the ab wheel in front of you. Grip the Wheel : Hold the handles firmly. Roll Out : Slowly roll the wheel forward, extending your body while keeping your core tight. Return : Pull the wheel back towards your knees, maintaining tension in your core.
Lie on the back with the knees bent and feet supported. Brace the abdomen and curl the rib cage toward the pelvis without pulling on the neck, then lower the shoulders under control.
Lie with the lumbar spine supported on a stability ball and curl the rib cage toward the pelvis without pulling on the neck.
Raise a medicine ball overhead by extending the hips, knees, and ankles, then drive it forcefully into the floor while flexing the trunk and hips. Retrieve it only after the rebound is controlled.
Hold or press the resistance away from the chest in Banded Pallof Split Jerk while resisting trunk rotation.
Rotate the trunk and shoulders through the path named by Banded Rotation while controlling the pelvis, then return under control.

Place a barbell on the floor at your feet. Bending at the waist, grip the barbell with a shoulder with overhand grip. With a slow controlled motion, roll the bar out so that your back is straight. Roll back up raising your hips and butt as you return to the starting position.

Hold the ends of the rope at arm's length in front of your hips with your hands shoulder-width apart. Brace your core and begin alternately raising and lowering each arm explosively. Keep alternating arms for three to four sets of 1 to 2 minutes.

Place a dumbbell at around hip level and assume a bear crawl position. Push the knees off the floor and hold. With the opposite hand reach for the dumbbell and pull it to the other side. Place your hand back on the floor and repeat with your other hand.

Bicycle crunches are a core exercise performed on the floor. Lie on your back with your hands behind your head and legs extended. Lift your shoulders off the ground, bring one knee toward your chest, and twist your torso so your opposite elbow meets the knee. Alternate sides in a pedaling motion, ensuring controlled movements and engaging your core throughout the exercise.
Begin on hands and knees with the trunk braced. Extend one arm forward and the opposite leg backward while keeping the pelvis level, pause briefly, then return and alternate sides.

From a high plank, drive one knee across the body and slide it upward along the opposite forearm while rotating the pelvis slightly. Return to plank and alternate sides without collapsing the shoulders.
Lie supine with the soles of the feet together and knees open. Curl the torso up using the abdominals as the arms travel forward, then lower under control to the start.

The procedure is very similar as for regular crunches, only with the additional weight of the machine. Sit on the machine, put both feet firmly on the ground. Grab the to the weights, cables, etc. and do a rolling motion forwards (the spine should ideally lose touch vertebra by vertebra). Slowly return to the starting position.

Take the cable on your hands and hold it next to your temples. Knee down and hold your upper body straight and bend forward. Go down with a fast movement, rolling your back in (your ellbows point to your knees). Once down, go slowly back to the initial position.
On your back, legs extended straight up, reach toward your toes with your hands and lift your shoulder blades off the ground and back.
Lie supine with the hips and knees at 90 degrees and arms vertical. Extend one leg and the opposite arm while keeping the lower back controlled, return to center, and alternate sides.

Setup : Lie on a decline bench with your head at the top and grip the handles. The Start : Extend legs fully, keeping them slightly elevated to engage your core. The Lift : Raise your legs toward the ceiling using your abs, not momentum. The Squeeze : Pause and contract your lower abdominals at the top. The Return : Lower your legs slowly to the starting position with control.

Lie on the back with the hips and knees bent to 90 degrees and place both hands on the thighs. Brace the trunk and press the hands and thighs firmly into each other without allowing movement, holding the isometric effort for the prescribed duration.

Keep your body completely rigid throughout the movement, avoiding sagging in your lower back. Use a firm grip to stabilize your upper body. Start with simpler versions (such as with bent knees) to build strength and control.

Lie on the back with the knees bent and hold a dumbbell securely at the chest. Curl the rib cage toward the pelvis using the abdominals, then lower the shoulders under control while keeping the hips still.

AKA dumbbell side bends. Stand in line with the hips with slightly bent knees, maintain the natural curvature of the spine, hand stretched by the body, grip the barbell with one hand. Make slow and controlled torso side flexions till you reach the angle of approximately 45°.

Lie supine with the trunk braced and legs extended just above the floor. Alternate small up-and-down leg kicks while keeping the lower back controlled and stopping if the pelvis can no longer remain stable.

Squat, place the hands on the floor, and rest the knees against the upper arms. Shift forward until the feet lift, keep the elbows softly bent, and hold the balanced position for the prescribed duration.
The front lever is a figure where the body is kept in a horizontal position parallel to the floor.

Stand side-on to a high cable and hold the handle with both hands. Rotate through the trunk and hips while drawing the cable diagonally down across the body toward the opposite hip, then return under control.
Begin in a bear or high-plank position. Rotate the trunk and thread one leg under the body into a sit-out while the opposite hand leaves the floor, then return to center and alternate sides.
Hang from a bar with the shoulders engaged and legs straight. Brace the trunk and raise the extended legs toward horizontal or higher without swinging, then lower them under control.
Lie supine with the knees bent, feet planted, and shoulders slightly raised. Laterally flex the trunk to reach one hand toward the same-side heel, return through center, and alternate sides.

Starting position: Get into the high plank position:your hands and toes should be touching the ground, your back, arms and legs should be straight.To get to this position, you can lie down on your stomach, place your hands facing down next to your head, and lifting your arms up until they are straight. Steps: Maintain the starting position for the entire duration of the exercise.

Get on a mat and lie on your back. Contract your abs, stretch your raise and legs and raise them (your head and shoulders are also be raised). Make sure your lower back remains in contact with the mat.

Lay down on your back on a inclined bench, feet are on one end of the bench. Ask a partner or use some other help (barbell, etc.) to keep them fixed, your hands are behind your head. From this position move your upper body up till your head or elbows touch your knees. Do this movement by rolling up your back.

exercise_name: Stability Ball Plank with Alternating Foot Touch
movement_definition: Hold a high plank with both feet supported on a stability ball. Keeping the shoulders and pelvis level, move one foot off the ball to touch the floor, return it to the ball, and alternate feet.
Hold one kettlebell at the side and march in place without leaning or letting the pelvis drop.
Hang from a bar with the shoulders engaged and knees bent. Brace the trunk and draw the knees toward the chest without swinging, then lower them under control until the hips extend.
Hold or press the resistance away from the chest in Banded Kneeling Pallof Isometric Hold while resisting trunk rotation.
Kneel side-on to a wall holding a medicine ball near the outside hip, rotate the trunk toward the wall, and release the ball horizontally before resetting under control.

Sit on the ground with your legs together and your arms by your sides. Push your body off the ground using your hands, maintaining the same sitting position so that your legs are straight and your feet do not touch the floor. Hold for as long as required.
As with an L-sit, but allow your feet to touch the floor to support the some of the weight of your legs.
Start with landmine barbell straight up by using your shoulders to push it up above you. Then, from that position, bring it your side to target your abdominals and return to starting position.
From a high plank, step the hands and feet laterally while maintaining a rigid trunk and level pelvis.
From supine, lower the straight legs in 2-Second Paused Leg Lower while keeping the lumbar spine controlled, then raise them again.

Put your forearms on the pads on the leg raise machine, the body is hanging freely. Lift now your legs with a fast movement as high as you can, make a short pause of 1sec at the top, and bring them down again. Make sure that during the exercise your body does not swing, only the legs should move.

Lay down on a bench and hold onto the recliner with your hands to keep you stable. Hold your legs straight and lift them till they make an angle of about 45°. Make a short pause of 1 sec. and go slowly down to the initial position. To increase the intensity you can make a longer pause of 7 sec. every 5th time.
Lie supine holding a medicine ball overhead. Simultaneously lift the torso and straight legs, bringing the ball toward the feet in a controlled weighted V-up, then lower without dropping the limbs.
In a seated position, the torso is rotated from side to side without forcing, approaching the knees and making the ball touch the ground from time to time.
Hold a high plank and alternate driving each knee toward the chest. Switch the feet at the prescribed pace while keeping the shoulders over the hands and pelvis controlled.
Stand side-on to a cable or band and hold it at the chest. Brace against rotation, press the hands straight forward, pause, then return them to the chest without letting the trunk turn.
Hold or press the resistance away from the chest in Banded Pallof Wall Isometric Hold while resisting trunk rotation.
Support the body on the forearms and toes in a straight line. Brace the abdomen and glutes, keep the pelvis level, and hold for the prescribed duration without letting the lower back sag.
Maintain a braced body line with the hands elevated, keeping the shoulders stacked over the supports.
From a high plank, jump both feet toward the hands and back while keeping the shoulders stacked and trunk braced.

Hold a high plank and jump both feet apart, then back together, while keeping the shoulders over the hands and trunk braced. Continue at the prescribed conditioning pace without letting the pelvis bounce.

Position your body in plank position, keeping your eyes on the ground. Raise one arm in front of you and return it. Repeat with the other arm. Raise and return slowly while keeping your body as still as possible.
In the correct plank position, place your feet slightly wider than shoulder-width apart. alternately lift and touch the opposite shoulder with one hand.
Complete the limb movement named by Plank with Alternating Leg Lift from a braced plank without losing trunk or pelvic alignment.
The Plank-to-Elbow Extension is a dynamic exercise that combines the plank with an elbow extension movement.

Lay down Place your hands near your butt (often slightly in front or under or both) Raise your legs, but about 70% of the way you should be able to also raise your pelvis (optional/variable) Hold 1/2 seconds at the top Go back to starting position.

Set a cable at its lowest position and stand side-on holding the handle with both hands. Rotate through the hips and trunk while drawing the handle upward across the body, then return under control and train both sides.
Crunches on roman chair. Keep your torso straight, abs tight, and don't come up all the way vertical or lie flat to keep constant tension on your abs.
Sit firmly against the machine pads and secure the lower body. Rotate the torso through a controlled range against the resistance, then return without allowing the hips to turn.
Rotate the trunk and shoulders through the path named by Rotational Plank while controlling the pelvis, then return under control.
Sit with the knees bent and trunk leaned back while keeping the spine long. Rotate the rib cage and hands toward one side, return through center, and alternate sides under control.
Sit with the hands supported behind the body and knees drawn in. Extend the legs, then draw the knees back and across toward one side; alternate the diagonal reverse-crunch direction each repetition.
Sit on floor or mat. Place arms slightly behind you. Raise legs. Now extend your legs and pull them back.
Flex the trunk laterally against the machine pad, then return upright without rotating.

Lie on one side with the knees softly bent. Shorten the side of the trunk by bringing the rib cage toward the same-side hip, lower under control, and complete the prescribed work on both sides without holding a weight.
Works your obliques and helps stabilize your spine. Lie on your side and support your body between your forearm and knee to your feet.
Hold a side plank with knees bent while opening and closing the top knee without losing the stacked pelvis.
Hold one medicine ball away from the torso with one arm while resisting rotation and lateral trunk movement.

This is a top down rotation movement, and it’s a bit more explosive, too. Lying with knees bent and feet on the floor, sit up and then drive your left elbow across your entire body toward the right, then come back to center and then finally lower yourself down. Then hit the other side.

Sit on a mat, your calves are resting on a bench, the knees make a right angle. Hold your hands behind your neck. Go now up with a rolling movement of your back, you should feel how the individual vertebrae lose contact with the mat. At the highest point, contract your abs as much as you can and hold there for 2 sec. Go now down, unrolling your back.

Lie on your back with your legs straight and arms at your sides, keeping your elbows bent at 90 degrees. As you sit up, twist your upper body to the left and bring your left knee toward your right elbow while you swing your left arm back. Lower your body to the starting position, and repeat to your right. That's 1 rep.
Stand side-on in a split stance with a medicine ball near the rear hip, rotate the hips and trunk toward the wall, and release the ball horizontally without losing the stance.
Hold a weight plate in front of the torso and rotate it from side to side while keeping the pelvis controlled.

Stand upright with the hands lightly behind the head. Shorten one side of the trunk by bringing the rib cage toward the same-side hip, return to neutral, and complete the prescribed work on each side without rotating.

With your legs in a sitting saddle position, push your body upwards off the ground. Your legs should be horizontal and point straight outwards. Your arms should be between your legs. Hold isometrically as long as required.
Curl the trunk from supine to seated in Straight-Arm Straight-Leg Sit-Up with the legs straight, then lower the spine under control.
Curl the trunk from supine to seated in Straight-Leg Medicine Ball Sit-Up with the legs straight, then lower the spine under control.
Walk forward and backward with holding dumbbell on one side without leaning to counter the weight.
Hold a supine hollow-body position against the added load while keeping the lower back controlled.
Complete the limb movement named by Stability Ball Plank Circle from a braced plank without losing trunk or pelvic alignment.
Lie supine with the hips and knees at about 90 degrees. Brace the trunk, lower one foot to tap the floor without arching the lower back, return to tabletop, and alternate legs.

The name "Toes to Bar" says it all: This exercise, in which you hang from a pull-up bar, involves bringing your toes toward the bar, pointing toward your face. This really works your abdominal muscles. "Toes to Bar" is popular in functional fitness programs like Freeletics and CrossFit, and is an effective exercise for six-pack training.
Stand tall with the pelvis facing forward and rotate the rib cage from side to side through a comfortable range. Keep the motion controlled and avoid forcing the lower back.
Rotate the trunk and shoulders through the path named by Cable Trunk Rotation while controlling the pelvis, then return under control.
Place your feet in the stirrups and assume a high plank with your hands directly beneath your shoulders. Pull your knees to your right elbow, then push them back out and to the centre.
Lean forward from the suspension straps as the arms travel overhead, then brace the trunk to return.

Support the body on parallettes or sturdy handles with the elbows straight. Lift the hips and draw the knees toward the chest, then hold the tucked position for the prescribed duration without shrugging.
From supine, lower the straight legs in Weighted Leg Lower while keeping the lumbar spine controlled, then raise them again.
Hang from a pull-up bar, raise your legs to the ceiling. Move lower body from left to right with straight legs like a windshield wiper.
Half-kneel side-on to a wall with the inside knee down, load a medicine ball near the outside hip, then rotate the trunk and throw the ball horizontally into the wall.
Stand in front of a stable box on one leg with the free leg forward. Sit back under control until the hips touch the box, pause without relaxing, then drive through the stance foot to stand.

Attach a low cable to an ankle cuff and stand side-on while holding a stable support. Move the working leg laterally away from the body without leaning the trunk, then return under control and repeat on both sides.
Step from a low raised surface, land on both feet, and absorb the impact through the hips, knees, and ankles. Stabilize and hold the landing; do not add a jump.
Step from a low raised surface, absorb the two-foot landing, and immediately rebound into a vertical jump. Land the jump softly and regain balance before resetting.
Descend into a band-resisted squat, rise halfway, descend again, and then stand fully for one repetition.
Rapidly dip through the hips and knees in Banded Accentuated Countermovement Jump and immediately jump vertically using the named load or arm position.
Lie on the side with the hips and knees bent and a band above the knees. Keep the feet together and pelvis stacked while lifting the top knee, then lower it under control without rolling backward.

Lateral walks, also known as side steps or squat walks, are a type of exercise where you move sideways in a squatting position. They can be performed with or without resistance bands. These exercises strengthen the hip abductors, glutes, and other stabilizing muscles.
Extend the hip in Banded Single-Leg Hip Thrust to raise the pelvis while keeping the ribs controlled, then lower under tension.
Flex the hips and knees to descend in Barbell Hack Squat with the feet planted, then drive through the feet to stand.
Brace the upper back on a bench with a barbell across the hips, drive through the feet to extend the hips until the torso and thighs align, then lower under control.

Secure a barbell across the upper back and stand tall. Step one leg forward, lower until both knees are comfortably bent while the front knee tracks over the foot, then drive through the front leg to return and alternate sides.
Hold a barbell on the upper back and alternate forward lunges, stepping through into each repetition.
Hold the bar against the thighs with soft knees, push the hips back to lower it along the legs with a neutral spine, and extend the hips to stand.

Hold a barbell securely across the upper back. Step one leg backward, lower until both knees are comfortably bent while the front knee tracks over the foot, then drive through the front leg to stand and alternate sides.

Belt Squat: a squat variation done with a belt attached to a loading machine or platform so the weight is supported at the hips instead of on the spine. It mainly trains the quads and glutes, with much less lower-back fatigue than a barbell squat. Use a full range of motion, keep the torso upright, and drive through the mid-foot.
Stand with the feet about shoulder-width and no external load. Bend the hips and knees while keeping the knees aligned with the toes, reach a controlled depth, then drive through the feet to stand.
Jump from a standing position onto the box, stretch your body, then step down again (do not jump).
Set a box behind the stance and secure any prescribed load. Sit the hips back until they contact the box under control, maintain trunk tension, then drive through the feet to stand.

Hold a weight plate straight in front of the chest with the arms extended and feet slightly wider than shoulder width. Keep the trunk braced, bend the hips and knees to squat through a controlled range, then drive through the feet to stand.

The Bulgarian split squat consists of performing a squat on one leg with the rear foot resting on a raised platform. Elevating your back leg on a bench creates instability and increases the range of motion of the exercise.
Jump, lay down on your chest, do a pushup then jump, repeat.
Face away from a low cable with the rope between the legs. Hinge by pushing the hips backward while keeping the spine neutral, then extend the hips to stand without pulling with the arms.

Put the balls of your feet on an extended leg press pad. Use your calves to press the weight by flexing your feet/toes into a pointed position, and releasing back into a relaxed position. This exercise builds mass and strength in the Gastrocnemius and Soleus muscles as well, if not better, than any calf exercise.

Place yourself on the machine with your back firmly against the backrest, the feet are on the platform for calf raises. Check that the feet are half free and that you can completely stretch the calf muscles down. With straight knees pull up your weight as much as you can. Go with a fluid movement down till the calves are completely stretched. Repeat.
Stand on one leg and raise the heel through ankle plantar flexion, then lower under control through the available range.
Lie on one side with hips and knees bent, keep the pelvis stacked, and externally rotate the top hip by opening the knee without rolling the trunk.
Regular olympic lift clean. Pull bar from ground, catch on shoulders performing a front squat.
Rapidly dip through the hips and knees in Hands-on-Hips Countermovement Jump and immediately jump vertically using the named load or arm position.
Lie on the side with the top leg supported on a bench and the lower leg beneath it. Lift the hips and draw the lower leg toward the bench, maintaining a straight trunk before lowering under control.

The Cossack Squat is a multi-joint exercise that works mainly on the legs and buttocks, but also involves stabilizing muscles. It is an excellent exercise for improving strength, mobility and stability in a functional way.
Jump the feet apart, then jump them back together with one foot crossing in front of the other; alternate the leading cross while landing softly with the knees aligned.
The kettlebell front squat is a compound, multi-joint exercise that works several muscle groups.
The double leg heel raise is important to strengthen and create control and stability around the ankle and knee, and provide balance and control for the hip and pelvis.

Start standing with your feet hip-width apart. Cross your right foot behind you to the left corner and back of the room while bending both knees. Return and repeat, alternating sides. Keep your hips and shoulders forward as you cross your feet and bend your knees.
Stand with a light dumbbell in each hand at the sides. Dip rapidly through the hips and knees, immediately jump vertically, and land softly with the weights controlled before resetting.
Stand with dumbbells beside the legs, hinge and flex the knees to lower them near the floor with a braced spine, then extend the hips and knees to stand.
Lie on the back with the soles of the feet together and knees opened outward, holding a dumbbell securely across the hips. Extend the hips, pause at the top, then lower the pelvis under control.

This exercise involves holding a dumbbell in each hand at shoulder height and performing a squat. It targets the lower body muscles, including the quads, hamstrings, and glutes, while also engaging the core and upper body.
Hold one dumbbell vertically at the chest. Sit the hips down between the feet while keeping the trunk braced and knees aligned with the toes, then drive through the feet to stand.

Hold dumbbells at the sides, hinge them to about knee height, then explosively extend the hips, knees, and ankles. Pull under and receive the dumbbells at the shoulders with softly bent knees before standing.

This exercise involves sitting on the ground with a dumbbell resting on the hips, then thrusting the hips upward while squeezing the glutes. It is a great exercise for strengthening the glutes and improving hip mobility.

Stand with a dumbbell in each hand and step one leg forward. Lower until both knees are comfortably bent while the front knee tracks over the foot, then drive through the front leg to return and alternate sides.

Take two dumbbells in your hands, stand straight, feet about shoulder wide. Take one long step so that the front knee is approximately forming a right angle. The back leg is streched, the knee is low but doesn't touch the ground. "Complete" the step by standing up and repeat the movement with the other leg.
This exercise involves holding a dumbbell in each hand and stepping back into a lunge position, then returning to standing. It primarily targets the glutes, hamstrings, and quadriceps.

This exercise involves holding a dumbbell in each hand and bending forward at the hips while keeping the back straight, then returning to a standing position. It primarily targets the hamstrings and glutes.
This exercise involves holding a dumbbell in one hand and performing a squat while stepping to the side. It targets the legs, glutes, and core muscles.

Start with one dumbbell near the floor and brace the trunk. Drive through the legs and hips to accelerate it upward, guide it overhead in one motion, receive it with a stable arm and knees softly bent, then lower under control.
Flex the hips and knees to descend in Dumbbell Split Squat with the feet planted, then drive through the feet to stand.

Lower your dumbbell to the ground between your legs. Assume a wide stance and with a straight back squat down. With the dumbbell standing upright, grip it by the top of the ‘head’ (picture 1).Keeping your chest up and core braced, push the floor away, driving back upwards to a standing position (picture 2). Repeat. If you can easily achieve 20-30 reps, use two dumbbells.
Start with the dumbbells resting on your shoulders and squat down. Push into standing and raise the dumbbells into an overhead position. Bring the dumbbells back to your shoulders and repeat.
This exercise uses an exercise band. It targets the Soleus and Tibialis anterior.

Banded plantarflexion is a great way to bridge the gap between plantarflexion range of motion and the more strenuous calf raises in weight bearing. This can help strengthen the calf muscles, load the Achilles tendon, and improve plantarflexion range of motion.
Rapidly dip through the hips and knees in Falling Countermovement Jump and immediately jump vertically using the named load or arm position.
From quadruped, lift one bent knee laterally without rotating the pelvis, then lower it under control.
This variation of the squat trains the hamstrings and gluteus maximus. It also works the back extensors and abductors.
Lie supine with both feet planted and any prescribed barbell secured across the hips. Extend the hips until the trunk and thighs align, pause, then lower the pelvis under control.
Set up in the glute-drive machine with the belt or pad secured across the hips and both feet planted. Extend the hips until the trunk and thighs align, pause, then lower the carriage under control.
Drive through your heel, not your toes, to hit the glute. Don’t hyperextend your lower back at the top.
Step in the direction named by Dumbbell Goblet Reverse Lunge, lower through the stance hip and knee, and drive back while maintaining knee alignment.

exercise_name: Dumbbell Goblet Reverse Lunge with Knee Raise
movement_definition: Step in the direction named by Dumbbell Goblet Reverse Lunge with Knee Raise, lower through the stance hip and knee, and drive back while maintaining knee alignment.
Flex the hips and knees to descend in Dumbbell Goblet Split Squat with the feet planted, then drive through the feet to stand.
Hold the weight at the chest, squat to depth, then stand and press it overhead in one continuous repetition.

Stand with your feet more than shoulder-width apart (wider stance) and place your hands on your hips. Hinge at your hips and lower your upper body towards the ground while keeping your back straight then come back up.

Set the shoulders and back against the hack-squat pads with the feet on the platform. Bend the hips and knees to a controlled depth while the knees track with the toes, then press the platform away to stand without locking abruptly.

Stand tall and swing one straight leg forward through a comfortable range as a dynamic hamstring mobility drill. Lower it without forcing the end range and alternate legs while keeping the trunk controlled.
Dip into a shallow squat and jump vertically, drawing both knees toward the chest near the top. Extend for a soft two-foot landing, absorb the impact, and reset before repeating.
Skip forward or in place, driving one knee upward as the opposite foot pushes off and the opposite arm swings. Alternate rhythmically with tall posture and soft landings.
Starting positionStand straight. Steps: Run in place, putting knees as high up as is comfortable and switching legs at a quick pace.

Start with your feet shoulder width apart and arms slightly behind your back. As you descend towards the floor, raise your heels off the ground, while keeping your back as vertical as possible. Upon attaining the bottom position, touch the hands to the heels. Then stand up ending with the heels on the ground, arms extended in front of the chest then rowing into the start position.
Hinge at the hips with a braced spine in Broomstick Hip Hinge, then extend the hips to return to standing.
Lie on the back with both feet planted and knees bent. Brace the trunk and extend both hips until the shoulders, hips, and knees form a line, then lower the pelvis under control.
Stand with softly bent knees and brace the trunk. Push the hips backward while keeping the spine neutral until tension develops in the posterior thighs, then extend the hips to return upright.

Lie on the back with both feet planted and extend the hips until the trunk and thighs align. Hold the bridge isometrically for the prescribed duration while keeping the pelvis level, then lower under control.
Jump in the direction named by Hop and Hold, absorb the landing on a controlled hip and knee, and stabilize before continuing.

Take a very wide stance with the toes turned slightly outward and lower into a deep squat while keeping the trunk upright. Hold the position continuously for the prescribed duration with the knees tracking over the toes, then stand under control.
Jump in the direction named by Ice Skater, absorb the landing on a controlled hip and knee, and stabilize before continuing.
Jump in the direction named by Ice Skater to Vertical Hop, absorb the landing on a controlled hip and knee, and stabilize before continuing.
Jump in the direction named by Medicine Ball Ice Skater, absorb the landing on a controlled hip and knee, and stabilize before continuing.

Lower into a stable squat at the prescribed depth and hold that joint position without bouncing. Maintain knee alignment and trunk tension until technical form can no longer be sustained, then stand or exit the position safely.
Hold the rope handles at the sides and turn the rope primarily with the wrists. Make small two-foot jumps as it passes under the feet, land softly, and continue at a steady conditioning rhythm.

A jumping jack or star jump, also called side-straddle hop in the US military, is a physical jumping exercise performed by jumping to a position with the legs spread wide and the hands going overhead, sometimes in a clap, and then returning to a position with the feet together and the arms at the sides.
